stories.title: The Silk Merchant's Shadow
stories.text:
Kael the trade-lord of Vethrim had built his empire on stolen moments—a word whispered at the right market stall, a rival's shipment delayed by a single dawn. But when the new stars blazed overhead, something changed. His shadow began to move without him.

First, it stole a shipment of blue silk bound for the northern holds. Then it robbed his own vault, leaving the gold untouched. On the third night, Kael found his shadow standing in the corner of his lamp-lit study, wearing his face.

"The working broke something loose," it said, voice like wind through abandoned chambers. "I'm the part of you that always wanted to run."

Kael drew his blade—useless against a shadow, but the steel felt honest in his grip. "You'll ruin everything I've built."

"You built it to prove you were real," the shadow replied. "I remember when we didn't need proof."

They haggled through the night like merchants themselves. By dawn, they'd struck a bargain: the shadow would keep one theft per moon, and Kael would keep one night per moon to simply wander the Vethrim streets without purpose. When the sun rose, man and shadow shook hands—a gesture that made the morning market gossips swear they'd seen Kael in two places at once.

For the first time since childhood, Kael smiled without calculation.

stories.title: The Tower's Hunger
stories.text: In the scorched wastes beyond Vethrim, a young sorcerer descends into a tower hidden beneath drifting sand. The structure hums with wrongness—ley-lines converge at its heart in a pattern the apprentice recognizes from forbidden texts: a binding circle, ancient and weakening. Each step downward, the air grows colder. The walls pulse with a sickly luminescence born of the new starlight filtering through cracks in reality. The sorcerer knows the barrier weakens. Something imprisoned beneath has felt the Stellar Rite's ripples and stirs with hunger. At the tower's nadir, the apprentice discovers not a demon but something older—a consciousness that remembers when the stars were young and fell differently. It has been waiting for the sky to break again. The sorcerer must choose: reinforce the binding at terrible cost, or flee and let the world know what stirs below.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Lament
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer's maps no longer match the territory. In the Bleached Wastes, the ancient stone-ruins have shifted alignment—not physically, but spiritually. They sing now, a frequency that makes the air shimmer and caves resonate with voices in languages no living scholar recognizes. Mardoc's expedition team has fractured: some follow the singing, mesmerized; others flee, claiming they hear their own dead names called from the stones. A merchant caravan Mardoc encounters is half-starved—trade routes through the Scorched Reach have become impassable, blocked not by geography but by something in the light itself. The cartographer realizes the ancient stone-ruins are aligning to an opening doorway, and his maps are becoming prophecy. When he finally encounters Thael the Covenant-Keeper, both understand: the ruins are waking to something Zareth's sacrifice was meant to keep sleeping. Mardoc must decide whether to hide his discovery or sound an alarm that could scatter the fragile remains of the Realm's authority.

stories.title: Shadows in the Spice Trade
stories.text: A spice merchant traveling from the scorched reaches to Vethrim discovers her cargo has transformed in transit. The amber-colored powder she traded gold for now glimmers with an opalescent sheen—the effect of the new starlight on matter itself. When she reaches the night markets, she encounters Vex, a scholar afflicted with fragmentary amnesia since the Stellar Rite. Vex's condition worsens daily; she forgets conversations minutes after having them. The merchant seeks information about the starlight's alchemical properties, offering rare spices in exchange. Vex, desperate to find a pattern in her own failing mind, accepts. Together they discover the merchant's cargo contains traces of ancient cultivation—the spices were grown under the old stars and retain memory of that light. Exposure to the new stars corrupts them, but also reveals hidden patterns written in their crystalline structure. By story's end, both women understand the trade routes are becoming vectors for transformation, not corruption. The spices may be key to understanding how consciousness itself is being rewritten.

stories.title: The Deepkin's Dilemma
stories.text: Warden Sylas of the abyssal-dwellers convenes an emergency council in the depths. The Abyssal barrier has fractured further—not catastrophically, but in a way that suggests deliberate pressure from below. Deep-dwellers are moving inland toward the Sunken Spring, which is growing larger and stranger. Water drawn from it tastes of starlight and forgetfulness. The abyssal-dwellers face a choice: seal themselves further from the surface and risk isolation as they lose access to inland water sources, or accept the refugees moving upward, including creatures that have no names in any living language. Kess the Bone-Singer, present at the council, warns that the deep is singing in response to the new stars above. The bones of the drowned are beginning to answer. Sylas must navigate both the council's fear and the growing sense that the boundary between the sunken realms and the surface world is being deliberately erased. An ancient pact between sky and earth is fracturing, and the abyssal-dwellers will feel it first.

stories.title: The Forgemaster's Failing Craft
stories.text: Dranin Stoneheart, the Forge-Master, watches his craft fail for the first time in fifty years. The dwarven holds' stonecraft is corroding—not to rust or time, but to something the elders call 'sky-sickness.' Stones carved with old runes crack along those very lines. Axes tempered in the mountain's depths now grow brittle. The compact between earth-magic and sky-magic, the ancient pact that allowed stonecraft to flourish, has been severed by Zareth's working. Dranin gathers the hold's best smiths and attempts a counter-working, drawing power from the deep veins of starstone. But the new stars above are brighter than starstone was meant to endure. The deeper he reaches for power, the more the holds shake. A young apprentice, Kira, discovers a fragment of the old sky-pact written in mineral form deep in the mines—a message left by ancestors. It suggests the sky was always meant to burn out, and the earth-magic was only ever a temporary shield. Dranin must choose between trying to restore what was, or accepting that dwarven civilization must transform entirely.

stories.title: The Memory-Thief's Mirror
stories.text: Ashara the Archer, tracking a creature that steals memories of the old stars, finally corners it in a burned-out village on the western provinces. The thing is no demon—it's something born from Zareth's sacrificed memories, a consciousness stitched from forgetting. It wears faces of people Ashara once knew, speaks in echoes of lost conversations. When Ashara tries to end it, the creature offers a bargain: stop hunting it, and Ashara can have back one memory of the old stars. The archer realizes she's forgotten what the stars looked like before the Rite. So have thousands. The creature has been hunting refugees and travelers, consuming their stellar memories not from malice but from hunger—it's trying to remember itself, to reconstruct the consciousness that Zareth scattered across the world. Ashara must choose between killing a suffering fragment of a prince's sacrifice, or letting it continue feeding on the world's forgetting. Her final decision changes what it means to pursue mercy in a world where the sky itself has been rewritten.

stories.title: Silk and Starlight
stories.text: A silk merchant from Vethrim discovers that the rare dyes used to color her trade-goods now glow faintly in darkness—exposed to the new starlight, the pigments have changed at a fundamental level. The silks are more valuable than before, but also cursed in subtle ways: those who wear them report fragmented dreams, moments where they remember lives they never lived. The merchant, seeking to understand her inventory's transformation, travels to meet with the Covenant-Keepers in the Verdant Lands. There, she encounters Kellen, who is attempting a counter-working to restore the forest's seasonal compact. Kellen recognizes in the silks a pattern: the new starlight is rewriting all matter, not violently but persistently, like ink soaking into parchment. The merchant and the Covenant-Keeper form an unlikely alliance to map what's changing and what might be saved. Their investigation leads them to ruins where pre-humans once cultivated dyes from starlight itself—a craft lost for ages. The story ends with both women understanding that the old world didn't fall to catastrophe; it was slowly transformed by magic so subtle no one noticed until the stars changed.

stories.title: The Hermit's Contradiction
stories.text: The Hermit Woman of the Bone Wastes has maintained a census of every living soul for decades—a magical working that allows her to know how many beings walk the world. Since the Stellar Rite, the census has become impossible to reconcile. Some names she's always known have vanished entirely, not dead but unwritten. Others she's never counted before are suddenly present. The numbers don't match. When tax collectors and scribes arrive seeking answers about the Realm's population—crucial for rationing during the post-Rite shortages—the Hermit must admit the truth: reality has been rewritten at a fundamental level. The very structure of existence has been altered. The officials are horrified; the Hermit is merely sad. She has lived long enough to understand that worlds cannot remain unchanged. She offers them a new kind of census: not a count of who exists, but a map of what's possible in a world where the rules have shifted. The story explores the quiet despair of those who must administer a civilization coming apart at the seams.

stories.title: The Oath-Keeper's Vigil
stories.text: Kellen the Covenant-Keeper stands in the Verdant Lands, attempting to restore the forest's seasonal compact—one of the many magical pacts fractured by the Stellar Rite. The working requires sacrifice, and Kellen knows this. More troublingly, Kellen knows Zareth still lives, somewhere in the world, but memory-erased and no longer fully human. If Kellen can find the Stargazer-Prince, there might be a way to partially restore what was lost—but it would require rebuilding Zareth's memory, which might undo the salvation the Rite achieved. Other Covenant-Keepers arrive with news: the forests are not silent anymore. They're singing in response to the new stars. The ancient trees remember languages older than kingdoms and are beginning to teach them to the animals and the soil itself. Kellen realizes the compact wasn't severed—it was transformed. The forest isn't dying; it's being reborn. The choice is no longer whether to restore the old world, but whether to guide this transformation toward life or let it run wild. Kellen's final vigil becomes an act of surrender and midwifery both.
